objections.objection: The implementation_steps schedule multigrade in-service training as 'year 1-4, recurring' while simultaneously assuming teacher-supply adequacy. This is a concrete mismatch: national data cited in the scenario (7,700 unfilled teaching posts and a halved graduate pipeline) and the scenario's own note that teacher-training-institute throughput is insufficient mean the proposed timeline lacks any workforce-supply or training-capacity accounting. There is no numeric plan for how many teachers must be re-skilled, no estimate of training-institute FTE expansion required, nor contingency staffing (itinerant specialists, accelerated certification, financial incentives). As written, the step risks rolling out multigrade pedagogy into understaffed schools, worsening the very staffing shortfalls it depends on rather than fixing them.
objections.suggested_revision:
Severity: high
Suggested revision: Replace the generic 'year 1-4, recurring' training line with a staged, capacity-linked rollout. Concretely: (a) within 3 months commission a teacher-supply and training-capacity audit that (i) estimates the number of multigrade classrooms to be created, (ii) quantifies how many existing teachers need multigrade certification, and (iii) models required throughput increases at teacher-training institutes; (b) budget and hire sufficient trainer FTEs to double current multigrade-training throughput within 24 months or create an accredited accelerated-certification pathway for experienced hires (with clear quality checks); (c) attach certified rural-posting financial incentives (bonuses/contract terms) and a temporary pool of itinerant specialist teachers/remote-teaching provision as contingency where local hires cannot be found; (d) phase the multigrade rollout as a pilot (e.g., 50 villages) in year 1–2 with rigorous staffing metrics before scaling to years 3–5. Tie each phase to measurable staffing targets (e.g., X certified multigrade teachers available per Y villages) and funding commitments before moving to the next phase.

objections.objection: The scenario proposes legal drafting (Ministry of Justice, year 1-3) enabling association/foundation-run schools and simultaneously schedules municipal pilot associations in year 2-4. This underestimates legal lead times, the need for funding-parity legislation, and administrative bandwidth to set up maintainers and governance structures. The text itself elsewhere admits the change requires maintainer-law redesign and documents precedent of rapid collapse (Gersekarát). There is no binding multi-year funding mechanism, no model contracts or escrow to prevent early collapse, and no municipal capacity-building grant — so the pilot timeline is infeasible and likely to fail for lack of statutory, fiscal and governance groundwork.
objections.suggested_revision:
Severity: high
Suggested revision: Recast the implementation_steps into a three-stage sequence with realistic legal and capacity timelines: (1) Year 0–1: legal-feasibility and risk assessment (6 months) plus drafting of a maintainership amendment that includes explicit funding-parity clauses and a statutory temporary transitional-funding guarantee for pilot sites; (2) Year 1–3: legislative passage and creation of standard maintainer contracts, model governance templates, and an association/foundation accreditation and audit regime; (3) Year 2–5: funded capacity-building grants for municipalities/associations (legal assistance, grant-management staff), binding multi-year (3–5 year) pilot funding placed in escrow to prevent early withdrawal (learn from Gersekarát), and a limited pilot (5–10 villages) with independent evaluation and mandated contingency plans (state takeover triggers if funding/non-compliance occurs). Extend the timeline accordingly (to at least 4–6 years for national scaling) and require a specific ministry budget line for maintainer transition support before any pilot is authorized.

objections.objection: The registry and mandatory reporting implementation timeline (design/legal/GDPR clearance year 1-2; district mandatory reporting year 2-4) is implausibly short because it omits two concrete legal and administrative preconditions: (a) a statutory obligation in education law to require districts to report the proposed disaggregated fields (SES, Roma/non-Roma, disability), which cannot be imposed by an administrative request alone without legal amendment; and (b) GDPR-compliant data-sharing agreements, IT procurement, and district-level staffing/training to collect and transmit sensitive disaggregated data. The scenario labels the evidence for registry absence as 'weak' but provides no prior audit of existing administrative systems (KIR-STAT, KSH), so the plan risks building an unfunded IT/policy mandate that districts will slow-walk or fail to comply with.
objections.suggested_revision:
Severity: high
Suggested revision: Rework the registry rollout into a legally-anchored, funded, and piloted programme with realistic lead times: (a) immediately commission a 6-month exhaustive audit of existing state/academic/NGO datasets (KIR-STAT, KSH, institutional repositories) to verify whether any closure/outcome registry already exists and what can be reused; (b) within year 1 draft and pass a focused statutory amendment (to Nkt. or an enabling law) that mandates the specific disaggregated reporting items and grants the registry legal basis for data collection and publication; (c) secure a ring-fenced multi-year funding line for registry IT build, data-analyst staffing, and district reporting-compliance grants; (d) run a 12–18 month pilot with 4–6 tankerületi centers to test GDPR workflows, ETL pipelines and district burden, followed by phased national roll-out in years 3–5 only after pilot success; (e) build enforcement/compliance mechanisms (audit schedule, penalties, and capacity-building support) and publish clear data-protection impact assessments before pilot start.
